titleStmt.title: Mr. Adams to to Mr. Seward..

div.head: Mr. Adams to Mr. Seward.
div.p: Sir: I have the honor to transmit copies of four sets of parliamentary papers recently published in connexion with the affairs of the United States, (Nos. 11, 12, 13, and 14.)
I have the honor to be, sir, your obedient servant,

frus:attachment.head: Return of claims of British subjects against the United States government from the commencement of the civil war to the 31st of March, 1864.

row.cell: Number of despatches and letters to and from foreign office.

Vessel Monmouth when on a voyage to Charleston, with alternative destination to Savannah, stopped by United States cruiser Niagara and warned off the whole southern coast on 12th of May, at which date the blockade is stated to have been incomplete.
Compensation refused; but the United States government have proposed to enter into a convention to submit this and similar cases to a mixed commission. Consideration of this proposal deferred.
Claim on account of Hilja, prevented from entering Charleston on. 12th May.
Susan G. Owens warned off southern coast by United States ship Niagara, and indorsement made on the register.
Informed, under advice of law officers, that the facts stated would not justify interference.
Part owners of cargo in Winifred of Virginia, captured off Cape Henry.
Cargo condemned, but Messrs. Phipps’s property restored on appeal.
Tobacco seized on board Hiawatha, and Crenshaw captured for breach of blockade.
Informed must apply to prize court.
Injury to property by United States troops.
Informed, under advice of law officers, that her Majesty’s government cannot interfere officially, but Lord Lyons would use his good offices.
Condemned. Sentence confirmed on appeal. Representation addressed to United States, government on special circumstances of the case.

cell.p: 2. Tobacco lying at Richmond, which he was prevented from exporting by the blockade.
3. Queen of England similarly detained at Richmond.
2. Informed, under advice of law officers, that her Majesty’s government could not interfere.

row.cell: Part owners of cargo of captured vessel Amy Warwick.
Messrs. Phipps’s share restored by prize court.
Owners of cargo captured in American vessel Forrest King.
Notice of blockade endorsed on register of Roscoe by U. S. ship Powhatan.
Refused to interfere, under advice of law officers.
Arrested when in charge of a bag of despatches from Consul Bunch.
Lord Lyons did not interfere, as Mr. Mure had been naturalized as an American citizen. He was released on the 18th of October. Correspondence laid before Parliament.
Detention of his yacht Gipsey, of New Orleans, by blockading squadron.
Vessel released after a few hours’ detention on the crew making a declaration of neutrality.
Condemned as enemy’s property. Refused to interfere, parties concerned being domiciled in southern States.
Seizure and detention of the Perthshire
1,000 dollars voted by Congress as compensation.
William Arthur seized at Portland as enemy’s property.
Vessel condemned. Refused to interfere, claimant having been domiciled in southern States.
Anna seized for alleged intention to violate coasting trade law.
Condemned. Refused to interfere pending appeal.

Released on Lord Lyons’s representation. As the United States government defended the necessity for such arrests, Lord Lyons was instructed not to prefer any claims at that time. Correspondence laid before Parliament.
Part owners of cargo of American vessel Crenshaw.
Seamen captured in H. Middleton and imprisoned in irons in Fort Lafayette.
Compensation refused, and as it appeared that the H. Middleton was a southern vessel Lord Lyons was directed not to repeat the claim.
III treatment by police at Baltimore in consequence of his wearing a likeness of General Beauregard attached to his watch-chain.
Case represented to United States government. Result not reported.
Boyne warned off the whole southern coast at a time when the blockade is stated not to have been completed.
Released on Consul Bernal’s representation. Right to British protection disputed.

cell.p: Released. Right to British protection disputed.
No claim made for compensation in any of these cases.

row.cell: Imprisonment on a charge of being on his way to join the southern army; a uniform being found in his baggage.
Released after investigation of the case.
45. Messrs. L. E. and G. Piers.
Susan warned off Hampton Roads by blockading squadron.
No case for interference, as it was not alleged that the blockade was ineffective.
Vessel and cargo released by prize court.
Refused to interfere; not entitled to British protection, his father having been naturalized, and he himself having exorcised the rights of citizenship.
No claim made on United States government, as he was released and did not apply for anything further.
Alliance and Gondar captured in the port of Beaufort, application to leave the port having been previously refused. Separate chum on account of Alliance having been warned off Charleston.
Condemned. Sentence reversed on appeal. Further appeal of captors to the Supreme Court pending.
Napier and Robert Bruce refused egress from the port of Wilmington by blockading squadron. Subsequently captured.
Had taken an oath renouncing his allegiance as a British subject. Lord Lyons instructed to represent the case to the United States government. Result not reported.
Indictment for conspiracy. Indictment dropped at Lord Lyons’s request.
Released on parole. Compensation refused. Correspondence continuing.
Inquiry promised. Result not reported. No claim made.
Result not reported. No claim made for compensation.
Did not interfere, as he had been naturalized.
Released. Refused to interfere further, under advice of law officers; Messrs. Maury having been concerned in carrying letters to and from the south.
Compensation not applied for, the detention arising in consequence of the regulations as to passports.
Captured in Adeline, and treated as prisoners of war. Compelled to sign a declaration not to repeat the offence of running the blockade.

cell.p: Conduct of commander of United States cruiser disapproved by United States government. Seamen set at liberty, and released from their declaration.

row.cell: Imprisonment at Key West. Captured in Victoria.
Telegraph captured and taken to Key West, and then released.
III-treatment at the time of the seizure of the Telegraph.
Condemned. Decree reversed on appeal. Further appeal by captors to supreme court pending; cargo restored to owners on payment of costs.
Capture of M. S. Perry or Salvor
Refused to interfere; Mr. Nolan having taken part in political discussions at St. Louis when the town was under martial law.
Vessel condemned, but restored on bonds being given for her value. Appeal against condemnation pending.
Capture of Andreita or J. W. Wilder
Refused to interfere; the vessel having been formerly a southern one, and not sufficient evidence being given of her bona fide sale to Messrs. Dexter.
Refused to interfere, under advice of law officers. Condemned.
Case tried in prize court. Vessel released. Compensation promised.
Southport fired at, detained, and searched
Lord Lyons refused to interfere further than by mentioning the case to Mr. Seward.
Timber at Pensacola which they desire to be permitted to export. Alleged misappropriation of a portion of it.
Permission refused. Further representation made to United States government. Answer not yet received.
Wages due to her deceased son, late a seaman in the United States navy.
Proceedings of military authorities in Kentucky.
131. R. L. Sanchez, Messrs. Tatham & Co.
Refused to interfere, as the search was not stated to have been conducted in an improper manner.
Condemned. Sentence reversed on appeal. Captors waived their right to further appeal on claimants giving up claim for damages.
Master and crew of Mersey imprisoned and ill-used.
Charges against United States officials proved to be unfounded.
Detention of Time at New York
Vessel detained as having coal on board, in accordance with act of Congress respecting such shipments.
Capture of British Queen and Active
Capture of Fair Play and Success
Capture of Intended, Coquette, and Providence.
A citizen of the United States who was acting as British vice-consul at Fredericksburg. Arrest and destruction of a quantity of flour, the property of Mr. Gemmill.
United States government made inquiry into the case, but refused redress.
Imprisonment and alleged forcible abduction from British territory.
Refused to interfere, was imprisoned for larceny, and did not adduce sufficient evidence as to abduction.
Refused to interfere. Law officers considered evidence insufficient for inter ference.
Destruction of York by blockading fleet off coast of North Carolina.
Claim for compensation tinder the con sideration of the United States government.
Refused to interfere; Mr. Cowan having been domiciled in United States, and exercised the rights of citizenship.
Vessel released by prize court. Compensation refused. Papers laid before Parliament.
Goods forwarded in China to New York; detained there and prevented from being shipped to Nassau without a bond being given that they should not be reshipped for blockaded ports.
United States government refused to remove restrictions. Papers laid before Parliament.
Capture of Emilie. Alleged misconduct of officers and men of United States ship Restless.
Annette detained by United States ship Rhode Island.
Explanation received from United States government.
Law officers advised that case should be tried in prize court. Condemned. Appeal not reported.
Detention of W. H. Clear off New York, and improper conduct of revenue officer.
Detention of goods at New York
Was passenger in the Memphis. Imprisonment.
Mr. Stuart instructed to use his good offices to procure Mr. Carroll’s release on his undertaking to leave the United States, or to find surety for good behavior.
Refused to interfere, his arrest having been caused by his entering into a discussion on the war with a United States officer.
Released. Refused to interfere further under advice of law officers.
Imprisonment for refusing to work on the fortifications at Newport, Kentucky.
Inquiry made. Instructions sent by United States government for the redress of all such cases on proof of nationality being afforded.
Destruction of Blanche by United States ship Montgomery, off coast of Cuba.
Compensation requested. Case still under discussion.
Passenger in Blanche taken in Montgomery to Key West.
Ellen; run down by United States ship Mercedita. Destruction of vessel and cargo and loss of effects.
842l. 9s. 7d. paid as compensation by the United States government.
Occupation of his house at New Orleans by United States troops.
Instructions given by the United States government that payment should be made for the use of the house.
Compensation asked for. Case under consideration of the United States government.
Imprisonment as a United States citizen. Was master of the Isabel.
Released by exchange. Lord Lyons instructed not to advance any claim, as Mr. Laurent had declared himself to be a citizen of the United States.
Lord Lyons instructed to ask for trial or release. Result not reported.
Collision between United States ship Vanderbilt and their vessel Symmetry.
Capture of Mont Blanc when at anchor off one of the Bahama cavs.
Vessel restored. Compensation promised. Correspondence continuing.
Refused to interfere, as it did not appear how the property had been destroyed, or by which army.
Capture of Comet, of which he was owner
Detention of Echo at New York to prevent intelligence being given of General Banks’s expedition.
Refused to interfere. Law officers considered that it was competent for a belligerent to impose such restrictions.
Lieut. governor of New Brunswick, through colonial office.
Imprisonment. Was supercargo of the Hiawatha.
Refused to interfere, as he was imprisoned for improperly gaining possession of part of the cargo and sending it to England.
Arrested when attempting to pass United States military lines.
Lord Lyons instructed to request moderate compensation. Result not reported.
Princess Royal captured off Charleston with contraband at the time of the attack on the forts.
Offer made to release on payment of expenses. Correspondence continuing.
Captured in Princess Royal and imprisoned.
The master regained possession of the vessel; she was, however, subsequently recaptured and condemned.
Refused to interfere, under advice of law officers, as he was domiciled in the United States.
Vessel released. United States government promise to ask Congress for compensation, the amount to be fixed by arbitration.
Injury caused to property by United States forces.
Compensation refused. Truth of his statements denied by United States authorities.
Loss from being prevented going to New Orleans in consequence of supposed intention of United States authorities there to arrest him.
Imprisonment and loss of property at New Orleans.
Compensation requested from United States government. Correspondence continuing.
Captured in Peterhoff. Alleged ill treatment in prison.
Waiting for judgment of court of appeal.
Captured in Peterhoff. Claim for detention and as part owner of the cargo.
Part of cargo released; vessel and remainder condemned.
Capture of Pacific; subsequently released without damages.
Refused to interfere, under advice of law officers, as owners should have appealed to a superior court.
Capture of Antona, of which he was master. Detention on board United States ship Pensacola.
Case tried at New York. Vessel condemned. Master released.
Part owner of cargo of St. George.
Released by admiral in command of United States squadron without going to prize court.
His vessel Dream fired at by a vessel hoisting United States colors.
Part owner of cargo of Dolphin
Referred to prize court Vessel condemned.
Imprisonment and banishment from Missouri for writing a political pamphlet.
Refused to interfere; the authorship of the pamphlet being proved.
Capture of Gleaner. Seized at Tortugas.
Released on arrival at Key West.
Detention of Castor at Bahia, by United States ship Mohican, at the instance of the United States consul.
Inquiry instituted. Result not yet reported.
Vessel condemned. Master and crew released.
Vessel condemned in Philadelphia prize court.
Owner of Clyde captured when on a voyage from Campeche to Havana.
Vessel restored. Cargo detained. Correspondence continuing.
Passenger in Clyde. Detention and loss of property.
311. Messrs. Pauvert & Son, W. H. Smith.
Imprisonment being in United States navy, for alleged refusal to fight against Great Britain.
Refused to interfere, as he was in the United States service,
Injury to property by United States troops.
United States government promise to in quire into such claims when money is granted by Congress for compensation.
Part owners of Cargo in Hiawatha
Vessel condemned. Application made on behalf of owners of cargo. Answer not yet received.
Mr. Hardcastle shot by a sentry at Old Capitol prison. Correspondence continuing.
Exaction of bonds from persons shipping goods from New York to the West Indies.
United States government refused to relax restrictions on this trade.
Isabella Thompson, captured between Nassau and Halifax.
Vessel restored without costs or damages. Appeal pending.
Refused to interfere, as Mr. Vernon had been domiciled in the southern States.
Captures of Julia, P. C. Wallis, Cuba, Southern Independence, Charlotte, Louise, R. O. Bryan, Defiance.
James Anderson seized by collector at Edgartown.
La Manche seized by collector at Edgartown.
On behalf of Mr. G. A. Trenholm, for the destruction of the Margaret and Jessie off the coast of Eleuthera, by the United States ship Rhode Island.
Evidence submitted to United States government. Correspondence continuing.
Prohibition to export cattle from New York to British West Indies.
Refused to interfere, as he confessed to having attempted to pass the military lines surreptitiously. Mr. Cosby released.
Part owner of cargo of Peterhoff
Informed must wait for result of appeal.
Vessel restored on payment of salvage. Cargo condemned.
Arrest and banishment from United States.
Restoration ordered by prize court. Appeal of captors pending.
} Captain, engineer and mate of Victor. Alleged ill-treatment at Key West.{
Refused to interfere, ill-treatment not being proved.
Vessel released without trial. Claim for damages under consideration.
Juno detained, fired at, and searched, by United States ship Kearsarge, off Terceira.
United States government have expressed regret for proceedings. Correspondence continuing.
Alleged improper exercise of jurisdiction by admiralty court at New York in an action at law brought against him by a seaman formerly serving in his ship, Dennis Hill.
Refused to interfere, as law officers considered the court had jurisdiction in such cases.
Vessel released by prize court, without damages. Refused to interfere at present.
Injury done by United States troops to property at Jackson.
Informed could not interfere, as he was domiciled at Jackson.
Part owners of cargo of Peterhoff
Alleged intention of the United States government to detain him in the United States.
Informed that ho should apply to Lord Lyons.
Atlantic, captured by the United States ship Princess Royal, and subsequently rescued.
Refused to interfere, as the vessel had been rescued.
Proceedings of the United States consul at Rio do Janeiro with regard to his vessels Gracie, Annie, and Lottie.
Waiting for report from her Majesty’s consul at Rio de Janeiro.
Released. Refused to interfere further, as he had exorcised the rights of citizenship.
Capture of Carmita, off the coast of Texas.
Etta, formerly confederate vessel Retribution, seized at New York.
Case to be tried in a court of law. Reason for seizure not reported.
Part owners of cargo of Magicienne
To be considered with other claims arising out of this case.
Ill usage when serving in New York police.
Refused to interfere, under advice of law officers.
Alleged misconduct of customs’ officers at Boston.
Capture of Saxon. Coals seized at Angra Pequena.
Released by prize court. Under consideration.
Detention of Florrie at New Orleans
Detailed to prevent intelligence of proposed expedition to Texas. Refused to interfere, such detention being justifiable.
Similar detention of H. G. Berry
Similar detention of Vigilant and other vessels.
Case represented to United States government. Did not interfere further. Lieutinant Rooke released.

Captures of Rebecca, Lacriola, Cora, Rowena, Sarah, Cataline, Morning Star, Chance, Caroline and Virginia, Emilie, Worster Shrubb, Volant, Emma, Dixie, Napier, Ladona, Aquilla, Elizabeth, Louisa, Josephine, Eliza, Fanny, Defiance, Fanny Laure, William Creevy, Nelly, David Crockett, Elmira Cornelius, Caroline, Emma, Wave, Brilliant, J. C. Roher, Princess Royal, Glide, Major E. Willis, Minnie, Brilliant, Time, Wanderer, Express, Wonder, Bettie Kratzer, Emma, Artist, Jupiter.
J. Gray killed by Lieutenant Donovan, of United States navy, on board Saxon.
{Interference of officer of a vessel flying United States colors with their wrecking schooner Dart.
Refused to interfere, as the case relating to land in Pennsylvania was one for the consideration of the United States courts of law.
Ill treatment and loss of property
Property destroyed by United States troops.
Alleged improper conscription, he claiming to be a British subject.
Imprisonment on board United States ship North Carolina.
Captures of Annie, Kate, Maria, and Bishop.
Owners of part of cargo of American vessel Lynchburg.
Messrs. Brown’s part of cargo released.

Captures of Adventure, Agnes, Avenger, Anna, Ascension, By George, Curlew, Dart, Director, Emma, Ellen, Eugenie, Edward, Florence Nightingale, Frolic, Goorgia, Hermosa, Handy, Harriet, Julia, Juniper, Justina, Lavinia, Lily, Linnet, Lady Maria, Margaret, Maggie Fulton, Martha Jane, Mail, Meteor, Maria Alberti, Newcastle, New Year, Neptune, O. S. Breese, Ripple, Relampago, Richard, Ringdove, Sea Bird, and Sea Drift.
Released. $17,150 damages and costs awarded against captors by prize court.

frus:attachment.p: Total number of despatches and letters to and from Foreign Office, 2,871.

frus:attachment.head: Memorandum.
frus:attachment.p: This return comprises the result of the fullest examination of the correspondence in the Foreign Office, and is believed to be complete as far as that correspondence goes. In order, however, to guard against any prejudice which might result to British claimants from their claims not being inserted in this list, it is necessary to observe that the return is not to be taken as conclusive; and this reserve is the more necessary inasmuch as it is understood that numerous claims have been registered in the British Mission at Washington, though not reported home, which may in due season be brought before the government of the United States.
